data.title: Coinbase: Betting On A Correction Is A Coin Toss
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Coming into 2025, Bitcoin and the broader market were declining, and then we saw prices bottoming in the first week of April, followed by a healthy correction. We're seeing the similar trend coming into 2026; however, the broader market seems to be holding up pretty well while crypto prices continue to witness massive drawdowns. Year-to-date, the (SPY) is up 0.5%, while Bitcoin and Coinbase (COIN) lost 21% and 27% of value, respectively. Coinbase's price trades closely to Bitcoin's price but with a higher volatility, as it is perceived to be a more risk-on asset compared to Bitcoin given the management and the operational risk embedded in the equity. The reason for the strong correlation between two assets has to do with crypto trading volume. Generally, when the Bitcoin price appreciates, we witness more trading activity among exchanges, which translates to more volume and more revenue. The opposite occurs when Bitcoin drops.

YoY, COIN lost 48.68% of its value, while Bitcoin lost 32.83%, and the S&P returned 14.23%. For the knife-catchers, the way to play the trade would be to buy Coinbase, as it's the stock that could witness steeper upside in the case of an industry correction. I wouldn't recommend such a trade for conservative investors like myself, as I see structural headwinds that limit the upside potential. If you want to play the "This could be the bottom" game, I think Bitcoin is a safer asset; hence, I'm rating COIN a hold.

Bear Case

The main bear case for Coinbase is that the firm still sources the majority of its revenue from retail trading volume. This is the business segment that has been in a cyclical decline since the peak of 2021. Institutional trading has also been in a decline but is slowly returning to the same level as in 2021. From Dec. 2021, retail trading volume decreased 55%, from 535 billion to 239 billion, and institutional trading volume only decreased 14%, from 1.14 trillion to 982 billion. The YoY growth rate in 2021 was over 600% for retail trading and over 700% for institutional trading; the second peak was witnessed in December of 2024, at 198.67% for retail trading and 145.55% for institutional trading. As of the last quarter, the retail growth rate is at 6.70%, and the institutional growth rate is at 1.76%, with signs of dipping into the negative territory as crypto activity continues to slow.

There are two reasons why Coinbase witnesses these volume declines, especially from the retail side. The first reason is that Coinbase still charges retail a higher take rate compared to its institutional client, 1.50% vs. 0.05% as of the last quarter. This opens a room for competitors like Robinhood (HOOD) and Hyperliquid to take some of its retail base by offering lower transaction fees.

According to Artemis Analytics, Coinbase, in line with Robinhood, charges a take rate well above 1%, while Hyperliquid charges below 0.1%. As a result, Hyperliquid has recently surpassed Coinbase in trading volume, proving that retail is willing to switch service providers to reduce costs, especially now that most crypto portfolios are in the red. I see this headwind not as cyclical but structural; what's only offsetting the decline is the slow adoption of DEX in the US due to lack of regulatory clearance.

The second reason is that crypto is losing retail market share to other trading/gambling avenues like sports betting, options, and prediction markets. Crypto has few use cases outside of trading, namely, cross-border payments and remittances, but trading still accounts for the majority of the use cases. The problem with trading as a use case is that the demand side is very fickle; once opportunities to make easy money disappear, so do the participants. To address this problem, Coinbase has decided to shift its mission from being a crypto gateway to being an "everything exchange" that will facilitate all kinds of trading on one platform. These are mostly matured markets with fierce competition, so for Coinbase to execute a good go-to-market strategy, it will have to get into an acquisition spree of smaller players that have already achieved product-market fit, e.g., the acquisition of Deribit, introducing capital and managerial risks into the equity.

Bull Case

The bull case for Coinbase is that the firm is relying less and less on trading volumes as the source of revenue and is growing its services segment, which brings high-margin recurring revenue. The impact of the services revenue on the top line is already apparent. Looking at the trading volume and the transaction revenue charts, we can see that the rebound from the 2023 bottom was subtle; however, Coinbase revenue rebounded significantly, and the main driver of the rebound was subscription and services revenue, which has been steadily increasing over the years. In December 2021, Coinbase revenue peaked at $7.84 billion on a TTM basis, with $6.84 billion coming from transaction revenue and $517.49 million coming from services revenue (7% of revenue share). As of the fourth quarter of 2025, TTM revenue is at $6.88 billion, with $4.06 billion coming from transaction revenue and $2.83 billion coming from services revenue (41% of revenue).

One headwind that is likely to face the services segment is that, as of the last earnings result, stablecoin revenue accounted for 48% of total subscription and services revenue, making this segment highly sensitive to interest rate changes. Given that there's a higher expectation of the Fed cutting rates in the coming years, this could impact Coinbase's topline growth.

Financial Analysis

Q4 2025 Income Statement

Looking at the last earnings result, Coinbase's revenue declined 22% YoY to $1.781 million, missing estimates by $46.1 million. This double-digit decline was caused by retail revenue decreasing 46% to $733.88 million and block rewards revenue decreasing 29% to $151.63 million. Offset by institutional revenue growing 31% to $184.95 million and stablecoin revenue growing 61% to $364.13 million. Aggregate transactional revenue declined 37% to $982.65 million, and services revenue increased 13% to $727.39 million.

Transactional expenses decreased 31% to $218.63 million, resulting in gross profits declining 20% to $1.56 billion and landing gross margins at 87.7%. Operating expenses increased 40% to $1.29 billion, resulting in operating income decreasing 74% to $273.75 million and landing EBIT margins at 15%, below the sector median of 24%. Finance expenses increased 103% to $1.16 billion, largely influenced by the price depreciation in crypto assets held on the balance sheet, which resulted in an operational loss of $886.31 million.

Full-Year 2025 Income Statement

Looking at the 2025 full fiscal year, transaction revenue increased 2% to $4.06 billion; the revenue growth was offset by retail revenue decreasing 3% to $3.32 billion. Services revenue increased 23% to $2.83 billion, bolstered by stablecoin revenue increasing 48% to $1.35 billion. Overall, total revenue increased 9% to $7.18 billion.

Operating expenses increased 41% to $4.73 billion, bolstered by the sales and marketing budget increasing 62% to $1.06 billion, resulting in operating income decreasing 38% to $1.44 billion, pretax income decreasing 48% to $1.52 billion, and net income decreasing 51% to $1.26 billion. Bulls would say these margin contractions are cyclical, and once crypto markets correct, margins will expand again. I see this as a structural headwind given the maturation of the crypto space, inviting more competitors offering lower take rates to retail.

Valuation

For the first quarter of 2026, analysts estimate revenue to be at $1.59 billion, implying an 18% revenue decline relative to the prior year quarter and further margin compression to the bottom line. I find these estimates to be fair, as bear markets tend to last more than 3 quarters. Given the structural headwinds I listed, Coinbase still trades expensively relative to its sector median, given the D- rating; however, most of its industry peers are concentrated in the F rating, proving that the premium valuation is not only idiosyncratic to Coinbase but also a broader industry mispricing.

Conclusion

Even though COIN closely tracks the price of Bitcoin, unlike Bitcoin, the headwinds for the firm are more structural and less cyclical, and it seems that the markets have priced this in, given the massive drawdown witnessed. Even if crypto markets recover, I am highly skeptical that this will immediately turn bullish for Coinbase as they continue to lose market share of retail trading amid the risks of rate cuts for that services business. I don't expect further drawdowns, as I think the stock has received enough market punishment, but I also don't see a massive correction for 2026; hence, a neutral position on this name is warranted.

data.title: Eurozone Industrial Upturn To Continue Despite December Dip
data.description:
By Peter Vanden Houte, Chief Economist, Belgium, Luxembourg, Eurozone

Eurozone industrial production fell in December, but the cyclical upturn is likely to continue on the back of stronger domestic demand

Structural headwinds won't stop the cyclical recovery

Eurozone industrial production fell by 1.4% month-on-month in December but still grew by 1.2% on a year-on-year basis. The month-on-month decline was largely expected, as the recently published German and French figures were weak for December.

It is easy to be downbeat about the prospects for eurozone industrial growth, as structural headwinds remain important. Natural gas prices are still more than three times as high in Europe as in the US, and cheap Chinese exports to Europe continue to grow at a strong pace. At the same time, European exporters still face higher import tariffs in the US. In the January business sentiment survey from the European Commission, the assessment of export orders remained downbeat, but overall orders improved. This suggests that domestic demand within the eurozone is starting to pick up, with German stimulus plans likely to be an important driving force. Indeed, German industrial orders climbed nearly 20% in the last four months of 2025.

There are also signs that the inventory correction is largely behind us, with the assessment of stocks now close to the historical average. We therefore expect manufacturing to contribute positively to eurozone growth this year, even though the structural issues are far from resolved.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: S&P Global: An Undervalued Dividend King For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) is a US-based leading provider of credit ratings, benchmarks, analytics, and workflow solutions to various markets worldwide. Founded in 1860, S&P Global is now a $133 billion (by market cap) financial markets behemoth that employs more than 40,000 people. Approximately 60% of the company’s revenue is derived from the US. The company reports results across five segments: Market Intelligence, 32% of FY 2024 revenue; Ratings, 30%; Commodity Insights, 15%; Mobility (which is being spun out), 11%; and Indices, 11%.

I view S&P Global as three wonderful businesses all in one: S&P Global has a scaled-up intelligence business with high-value proprietary data; it controls the largest ratings agency in the world; and it owns an irreplaceable indices business with various lionized S&P products under its umbrella (such as the venerable S&P 500, the global gold standard). Each business on its own would be very powerful and highly appealing for almost any long-term investor, but all three being combined into one package is what makes S&P Global one of the most unique and compelling long-term investment opportunities out there.

In data-driven markets where information is prized for insight, S&P Global has a treasure trove of proprietary data at unparalleled scale. Since companies issuing public debt are practically required to have a rating, and since there are only two global players providing these ratings at a trusted scale, S&P Global operates within a comfortable duopoly (with S&P Global estimated as commanding a market share of nearly 50%, and its only other major competitor commanding an estimated 30%).

And its indices business has numerous recurring revenue drivers all on its own, including subscription fees, asset-linked fees, and transaction royalties – and all of these are firmly embedded within global capital markets in such a way that it is almost impossible to displace. These are three formidable businesses on a standalone basis, but S&P Global has combined them into a complementary flywheel.

In a world where cycles are shorter and disruption is happening faster, the kind of durability that S&P Global possesses by combining three almost-unassailable businesses under one roof means it’s hard to imagine a world decades into the future in which S&P Global is not larger, more profitable, and more successful than it is today. To the contrary, I believe it’ll continue to pull its many levers to generate substantial revenue, profit, and dividend growth over the decades ahead.

Dividend Growth, Growth Rate, Payout Ratio and Yield

To that point, S&P Global has already increased its dividend for 53 consecutive years. Incredible. That’s one of the longest dividend growth streaks in existence. It easily qualifies S&P Global for its status as a vaunted Dividend Aristocrat and Dividend King. If anything, this is the Dividend Aristocrat (it’s this company’s index, after all). Its 10-year dividend growth rate of 11.3% is very solid, especially considering how that had come after 40 prior years of dividend growth already.

However, more recent dividend raises over the last few years have been much more modest as a result of acquisitions designed to strengthen the company’s grip over global markets (which was already strong before). A payout ratio of 28.2% shows us an extremely healthy dividend poised to grow at a high rate over the coming years.

This foundation is further reinforced by the business’s high rate of growth. Pairing such a low payout ratio with a business growing at a very rapid rate are dual tailwinds positioning shareholders for huge dividend growth over the years ahead. If there’s a drawback here, it’s probably the stock’s yield of 1%.

That’s not exactly a mouthwatering yield, but it is 20 basis points higher than its own five-year average. There’s more immediate yield than usual to be had here, along with a nice setup for dividend growth now that S&P Global is on the other side of some heavy acquisitive activity. This is looking like an opportune moment to invest in a king among kings.

Revenue and Earnings Growth

As opportune as it may be, though, we’re largely relying on past data. However, investors must constantly be considering the future, as the capital of today ultimately gets risked for the rewards of tomorrow. As such, I’ll now build out a forward-looking growth trajectory for the business, which will be taken into account during the valuation process.

I’ll first show you what the business has done over the last decade in terms of its top-line and bottom-line growth. And I’ll then reveal a professional prognostication for near-term profit growth. Lining up the proven past with a future forecast in this manner should give us the ability to roughly estimate where the business may be going from here.

S&P Global moved its revenue from $5.7 billion in FY 2016 to $15.3 billion in FY 2025. That’s a compound annual growth rate of 11.6%. Excellent top-line growth, although a meaningful jump in revenue occurred after S&P Global acquired IHS Markit in 2020 for $44 billion in an effort to significantly expand its data, increase recurring data, and open up cross-selling opportunities.

Meanwhile, earnings per share grew from $7.94 to $17.83 (adjusted) over this period, which is a CAGR of 9.4%. The IHS Markit acquisition muddies the waters, making a straight-line comparison somewhat inaccurate. If we zoom in and look at the most recent quarter (Q4 FY 2025), which is more current and relevant, S&P Global powered ahead with 14% YOY adjusted EPS growth. That’s a bit more like it.

Looking ahead, CFRA is projecting a 12% CAGR for S&P Global’s EPS over the next three years. That includes S&P Global’s upcoming spin-off of its Mobility unit (one of its smallest), which focuses on automotive intelligence. CFRA highlights S&P Global’s dominant position in credit ratings, its standing as the benchmark in indices, and its proprietary data. S&P Global has unrivaled brand power in not one but two industries (ratings and indices).

This is the index company. This is the ratings agency. Its data is the data. In my view, its three main businesses are nearly unassailable. Of course, there is the elephant in the room: AI. While CFRA concedes that questions remain regarding just how competitive and valuable proprietary data will be in an age of AI, S&P Global is busy embedding AI in products and partnering with LLMs in order to fortify the business.

How much of a friend or foe AI will be remains to be determined, but I can’t imagine we’re going to live in a world in which all IP is just scraped freely by third-party apps. And there’s much here that AI simply cannot do. Nobody is going to just “vibe code” the S&P 500. If we assume that CFRA is in the ballpark with its 12% number, that positions S&P Global for mid-teens dividend growth over the coming years by virtue of the payout ratio being so low.

Again, the 1% yield isn’t much, but those who appreciate high-quality compounders should like what they see. S&P Global’s stock has a 10-year CAGR (including reinvested dividends) of 17.7% – even after the recent drawdown – so it’s clearly been (and appears to remain) a high-caliber long-term investment for those willing to accept a low starting yield.

Financial Position

Moving over to the balance sheet, S&P Global has a great financial position. The long-term debt/equity ratio is 0.3, while the interest coverage ratio is nearly 20. Its credit ratings (by the other agencies) are well into investment-grade territory: A3, Moody’s; A-, Fitch. While the balance sheet isn’t quite the indomitable fortress it was a decade ago, it’s still a pillar of strength.

Profitability is robust. Return on equity has averaged 226.2% over the last five years, while net margin has averaged 29%. As impressive as these metrics are, they were even better before the IHS Markit acquisition. ROIC was reliably over 40% before that acquisition, for example, which is otherworldly. S&P Global had some room for a mistake here, so time will tell how much post-AI erosion occurs.

The good news is that the indices and ratings businesses are so good that any possible shareholder value destruction can be absorbed over time. Overall, from what I see, S&P Global is one of the best companies on the planet. And with economies of scale, network effects, barriers to entry, “sticky” products already firmly embedded into global markets, IP, and R&D, the company does benefit from durable competitive advantages.

Of course, there are risks to consider. Litigation, regulation, and competition are omnipresent risks in every industry. S&P Global is somewhat insulated from competition, as it operates (or even dominates) within oligopolistic markets. In addition, because of the regulatory hurdles involved in the global financial markets, along with the way in which S&P Global is so entrenched already, regulation is, arguably, as much of a shield as it is a risk for S&P Global.

The company’s massive trove of data could become less useful and valuable as AI-powered research becomes ubiquitous, although AI is trained on data and proprietary data/IP can’t simply be stolen. AI is currently considered to be a general, broader risk to this entire industry, but it’s unclear exactly what AI can do to dislodge embedded global indices and/or ratings agencies.

IHS Markit was S&P Global’s largest acquisition ever, and there are unanswered questions regarding just how synergistic and beneficial the deal was, along with whether or not the price paid was appropriate. The number of public companies has been decreasing for years, reducing opportunities for listings and ratings, but deal flow appears to be picking up after a lull (which increases the opportunity set for ratings).

Being a global enterprise, S&P Global does face geopolitics and currency exchange rates; however, its lack of physical infrastructure and the embedded nature of its financial products protect the firm from most geopolitical storms. Other than the IHS Markit questions, the risk profile of S&P Global does not strike me as high.

But with the stock in a severe drawdown – dropping by nearly 25% YTD alone – there seems to be a lot of consternation and worry now being priced into shares…

Valuation

The stock now has a P/E ratio of 21.9, based on adjusted EPS. That is nearly half of its own five-year average P/E ratio of 38.5. Its cash flow multiple of 19.6, which is not demanding at all even in isolation, is far lower than its own five-year average of 30.7. And the yield, as noted earlier, is higher than its own recent historical average.

So the stock looks cheap when looking at basic valuation metrics. But how cheap might it be? What would a rational estimate of intrinsic value look like?

I valued shares using a dividend discount model analysis. I factored in a 10% discount rate, a 10-year dividend growth rate of 15%, and a long-term dividend growth rate of 8%. The model includes a near-term acceleration in dividend growth relative to the proven 10-year dividend growth rate, but that 10-year rate has been negatively impacted by unusually small dividend raises over the last several years.

If we go back to before the IHS Markit acquisition, S&P Global was handing out 20% dividend raises like candy. I’m basically assuming a return to the status quo as it pertains to long-run dividend growth out of S&P Global. With the major acquisition already integrated, a low double-digit EPS growth rate being projected over the next few years, and the payout ratio being as low as it is, S&P Global has plenty of headroom to deliver a mid-teens type of dividend growth rate from here.

Of course, I wouldn’t expect that kind of growth to persist forever, and the model does allow for a natural slowdown into a high-single digit level, which I think is appropriate for the quality and growth that S&P Global exudes. The DDM analysis gives me a fair value of $376.75. The reason I use a dividend discount model analysis is because a business is ultimately equal to the sum of all the future cash flow it can provide.

The DDM analysis is a tailored version of the discounted cash flow model analysis, as it simply substitutes dividends and dividend growth for cash flow and growth. It then discounts those future dividends back to the present day to account for the time value of money, since a dollar tomorrow is not worth the same amount as a dollar today. I find it to be a fairly accurate way to value dividend growth stocks. While this has consistently been a very expensive stock, I think it’s now at least in the realm of fairness after an epic drawdown.

But we’ll now compare that valuation with where two professional stock analysis firms have come out at. This adds balance, depth, and perspective to our conclusion.

Morningstar, a leading and well-respected stock analysis firm, rates stocks on a 5-star system. 1 star would mean a stock is substantially overvalued; 5 stars would mean a stock is substantially undervalued. 3 stars would indicate roughly fair value. Morningstar rates SPGI as a 5-star stock, with a fair value estimate of $570.00.

CFRA is another professional analysis firm, and I like to compare my valuation opinion to theirs to see if I’m out of line. They similarly rate stocks on a 1-5 star scale, with 1 star meaning a stock is a strong sell and 5 stars meaning a stock is a strong buy. 3 stars is a hold. CFRA rates SPGI as a 3-star “HOLD” with a 12-month target price of $465.00.

I came out low, surprisingly. Averaging the three numbers out gives us a final valuation of $470.58, which would indicate the stock is possibly 20% undervalued.

Bottom line: S&P Global Inc. is a world-class company made up of three formidable businesses which have been complementarily combined into a fortress that’s nearly unassailable. The fundamentals are among the best I’ve seen. It’s the Dividend Aristocrat. With a market-like yield, double-digit dividend growth, a very low payout ratio, more than 50 consecutive years of dividend increases, and the potential that shares are 20% undervalued, long-term dividend growth investors would be wise to seriously consider this Dividend King now.

Note from D&I: How safe is SPGI’s dividend? We ran the stock through Simply Safe Dividends, and as we go to press, its Dividend Safety Score is 99. Dividend Safety Scores range from 0 to 100. A score of 50 is average, 75 or higher is excellent, and 25 or lower is weak. With this in mind, SPGI’s dividend appears Very Safe with a very unlikely risk of being cut.

Disclosure: I’m long SPGI.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cheesecake Factory Q4 Earnings Preview: Fighting The Wrong Enemy
data.description:
The last time I checked Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) was a year ago, and I told you that this full-service restaurant chain was fully priced after strong results in FY 2024.

A whole year has passed, and this operator with over 250 menu items definitely didn't ride the 'full-service mania' from January to July of last year.

The reason was simple—they just jumped in too late.

While other peers like BJ's Restaurants (BJRI), Applebee's (DIN), and especially Chili's (EAT) had already launched value menus at the beginning of the year, Cheesecake Factory launched it in July.

This likely caused the stock to remain flat since the start of FY 2025, with the gains from Flower Child/North Italia already priced into the stock after the 2024 bull run.

I think the performance of my last 'Hold' rating explains this.

Since that last piece, the stock has returned a little over 9%, trailing the S&P's 13% over the same period.

The stock is hovering a bit above the $53 to $56 price range I penciled in at the time.

The market is divided between considering Cheesecake Factory a top-tier operator with value levers (one of the investment cases I flagged in my Ultimate Guide for Restaurants in 2026) or a growth story driven by the incubation brands like FRC and Flower Child, with the core Cheesecake Factory and now North Italia anchoring the valuation and keeping it from a full re-rating.

That’s why an activist floated the idea of ​​a spin-off some time ago.

My Take on the New Strategy

Just take a look at same-store sales for The Cheesecake Factory, and you can see that same-store sales have slowed down quite a bit recently—especially in the second half of last year.

They had been using the same playbook as chains that seek to maintain traffic based on perceived value, such as Texas Roadhouse (TXRH).

In the case of Texas Roadhouse, they do the basics well: value meals (early dine, family meals), a focus on consistency, and price increases below inflation.

In this sense, management kept pricing slightly below inflation (between ~2 and 3% year-over-year) and expected that this would be enough to keep guests coming back. As most restaurants are located in upscale areas (shopping malls being a classic example), they gambled that there would be no trade-down.

But unlike First Watch (FWRG), which tried a similar playbook and is now seeing traffic rebound, Cheesecake Factory has seen comps stall, with a slight dip in traffic that was only offset by low single-digit pricing.

With middle-class confidence slipping and real wages getting squeezed by sticky inflation and slower pay growth, it makes sense that when other casual dining chains rolled out value platforms, some guests traded down from upscale spots to more low-priced casual options.

I think they really felt the pinch before changing their strategy.

Everything happened in July (so it also affected the Q3 trends), when they launched items ranging from $10 (snacks and small dishes) to $16.

By putting a 'Goulart Restaurant Stocks lens' on you, you can see:

  • Appetizers usually aren't the go-to option for price-sensitive guests. They're more of an add-on for people already ordering full-priced entrées. This is the complementary strategy that Olive Garden (DRI) is running to complement other value offerings, but it doesn't bring back the guests who traded down.
  • Bowls seem to be their best bet for value. Prices here (excluding the vegan option, which is slightly cheaper) range from $15 to $18, and they come quite generously filled, generally outperforming Chipotle (CMG) and Sweetgreen (SG) offerings, but they fall short in total price.
  • The types of offerings and price points give me the feeling that they are competing more with fast-casual restaurants than other casual dining.

These offers hit the menu halfway through Q3, and by now the results are already known to us.

Traffic didn't get the lift they probably expected, with comps slowing from 1.2% to 0.3%, and Q4 is shaping up around flat to down 1%.

If you ask me, I have a few clues as to why this happened.

Firstly, Cheesecake Factory is known as a typical full-service experience—generally with dishes that are a bit more elaborate than typical casual dining and group dinners—something that completely departs from the quick bowl-and-go occasion, especially during the day.

I also missed cross-selling options that would increase the order value, such as drinks or sides.

That would boost the value-per-dollar equation, as well as give the impression that it's a complete meal at a fixed price, and not just a cheap bowl.

Even BJ's 'Pizookie Meal Deal' for $13—also an upscale casual dining option—offers a full meal and a Pizookie for dessert with levers for cross-selling salad or chips for an additional $4.

Peeking at the Thesis Growth Engines

Those who have followed my previous articles on this restaurant chain will know that we can subdivide the brand portfolio between Cheesecake Factory on one side and North Italia, Flower Child, and other FRC brands on the other.

And that's precisely why the stock generally trades above other full-service restaurants (~15x EBITDA versus Brinker's 10x).

The growth thesis embedded here is that management will continue rolling out these other brands (generally a chunk of the 26 openings expected for FY 2026) and scaling them since construction costs are lower than a Cheesecake Factory.

I know I've already given this example, but it doesn't hurt to mention it again.

Look at Flower Child, for example. It generates an AUV of ~$4.8 million and boasts a restaurant-level margin of 17.4% (I took this data from Q3 FY 2025). Assuming the restaurants cost $2 million to build (assuming $380 per sq ft and a typical 5,250 sq ft restaurant), we would arrive at an incredible ROI of 41% and a payback period of just over two years.

If they can continue scaling margins and AUVs by scaling same-store sales, this could improve even further.

North Italia, however, is in a slightly different phase.

The Italian-American Cheesecake Factory concept has reported declining same-store sales and slightly compressed AUVs (~$8 million) for three consecutive quarters. Even so, they managed to pull out of the hat a small increase in restaurant-level margins, settling them at 15.7%.

There's not much of a secret here.

What has stalled North Italia's growth recently seems to be the weaker macro environment regarding upscale restaurants.

But you might be scratching your head and wondering if they get a higher ROI by opening different concepts from Cheesecake Factory, why don't they just maximize it by focusing on these other brands?

Well, it's a bit more complicated than it seems.

Today they have a small base, in top-tier markets and select locations. Expanding them, besides the risk of cannibalization—you saw how this works with Kura Sushi (KRUS) and other slightly more expensive concepts—they lose the novelty effect.

Not every concept is made to scale, and that might even be a painful truth.

With a portfolio more tied to upscale or fast-casuals (which is a highly competitive segment), Cheesecake Factory could become increasingly sensitive to the macro environment, and I'm sure the shareholder doesn't want that.

Still a 'Hold' At These Levels?

For Q4 FY 2025, management didn't give us one of its most encouraging guidelines.

For revenue, for example, they are targeting something between $940 and $955 million (slightly above the $921 million of the same period last year), but likely with same-store sales falling in the low single digits.

They also disclosed that they expect a net profit margin of 4.9% for the entire FY 2025. Doing the math here, if we consider midpoint sales for Q4, they would deliver total revenue of $3.73 billion and net income of $183.1 million (EPS of ~$3.67 based on 49.8 million shares outstanding).

Remember that the market expects an EPS of $3.75. So, if they actually hit that margin they projected, Cheesecake Factory would need to repurchase at least 900,000 shares in Q4 alone to achieve it and not miss.

There's a risk of it not being a beat in EPS in my opinion.

For FY 2026, early guidance predicts 26 new restaurants, with the largest chunk in the second half of the year. They also expect revenue growth of 4-5% ($3.87-$3.91 billion for the whole of FY 2026), likely accounting for weak same-store sales at the beginning of the year and accelerating in the second half.

The Wall St. consensus is a bit less conservative, targeting $3.91 billion.

Since they also targeted net income at ~5% in FY 2026, we could expect net income of $194.5 million and EPS of $3.98 assuming 2% fewer shares outstanding (slightly more than the 5-year average).

Comparing it to other full-service operators such as Brinker and BJ’s, where earnings growth has picked up, a multiple between 15x and 17x doesn’t look stretched.

This would give us a price target between $59 and $67 per share ($63 at the midpoint), an upside of only 8.5% at the levels where the stock is trading today.

That being said, I'm maintaining my 'Hold' rating for The Cheesecake Factory at the start of this year.

data.title: StandardAero: Carlyle Exit Pressure Masks Strong Fundamentals
data.description:
StandardAero (NYSE:SARO) has gained 21.7% since my last report on a largely flat performance of the S&P 500. It highlights the growth prospects for companies such as StandardAero in the aerospace and defense industries. The stock price actually went up around 37% before shareholder selling, which I pointed out as a primary risk to the share price, induced downward pressure on the stock price. In this report, I review that shareholder selling activity, assess the expectations for the fourth quarter, and update my price target ahead of earnings.

Why StandardAero Stock Price Retreated

The aerospace and defense industry has had a fairly good run-up since December 2025 on the prospects of higher defense budgets and regional turmoil regarding Venezuela. StandardAero also benefited from that, even though military sales have been the lowest contributor to sales over the last nine months (10-Q, Page 12), and with a high-single-digit growth rate expected for 2025, it is also the lowest-growth business for StandardAero. So, some share price pressure comes from a broader market and industry movement. However, there is also some selling pressure specific to StandardAero. This pressure is not related to the company’s operational performance but related to the company’s ownership. To understand this, we need to dig a bit deeper into Standard Aero.

Carlyle acquired StandardAero in 2019 for $5 billion and intended to find a buyer for the company, valuing the company at a $10 billion enterprise value. However, no buyer was found, and the company brought StandardAero to the stock market in an IPO in 2024. Today, the company has a $12.7 billion enterprise value. So, I would say Carlyle was not wrong valuing the company at $10 billion.

Following the IPO, Carlyle and GIC, which often invests in Carlyle-managed funds, owned 63% and 14% of the company, respectively. Given that the company’s earlier intention was to sell StandardAero, it is rather clear that the company aims to bring its ownership down, and it has been doing so in prior quarters. Carlyle’s ownership went down from 63% to 46% by Q3 2025, and GIC’s ownership declined from 14% to 10% as both owners incrementally sold shares.

In January, StandardAero announced that affiliates of Carlyle and GIC intended to sell 50 million shares with the option to sell another 7.5 million shares. At the same time, StandardAero would repurchase $50 million worth of shares.

On the 29th of January 2026, StandardAero announced that ownership by Carlyle and GIC was reduced by 57.5 million shares and 1.64 million shares were repurchased. This brings the estimated share of Carlyle down to 32% from 46%, and GIC’s ownership would fall from 10% to 7%, assuming that the reduction in ownership happened on a pro-rata basis. It leaves 61% of the ownership now to other parties. I believe that these two companies could bring ownership down further, potentially with a full exit in mind. So, additional share price volatility from existing shareholders selling may occur from time to time, but I would say it is the expected playbook and not indicative of a shareholder selling due to operational performance. If anything, Carlyle is capitalizing on the success of StandardAero as intended.

What Analysts Expect For StandardAero In Q4 2025

StandardAero will report its fourth quarter and full-year results on the 25th of February 2026. For the fourth quarter, analysts are expecting 12.4% growth in revenues to $1.58 billion, with estimates ranging from $1.57 billion to $1.61 billion. Analysts are expecting earnings per share of $0.24, with estimates ranging from $0.23 to $0.26. The company has beat estimates on revenues each quarter since it went public. On earnings per share, the performance is mixed, with two beats, two misses, and one quarter with in-line earnings per share.

The company has already issued preliminary results indicating between $6.053 billion and $6.083 billion with adjusted EBITDA of $806-$812 million and free cash flow of $200-$210 million.

StandardAero Stock Upgraded To Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For StandardAero, I am increasing the rating from "buy" to "strong buy" as ownership of Carlyle and GIC has further reduced, and we note that the company is trading at a discount compared to peers while upside against earnings for 2025 remains. Estimates for EBITDA and free cash flow have largely remained unchanged. The stock price target has increased modestly from $42.02 to $42.28. However, we note that growing free cash flow allows the company to either direct funds to acquisitions or shareholder returns. The company announced a share repurchase program of $450 million. Currently, I have only modelled in $50 million in share repurchases for 2025, keeping the assessment conservative.

As the company grows the business, we see that margins are expected to increase, cash conversion will improve, and capital intensity will decrease. At the same time, we see that the company’s net debt leverage is expected to be in an acceptable range by 2026 absent of share repurchases. The cash balance is expected to grow, and while the company has announced a share repurchase program, I do believe that share repurchases need to happen with the net debt leverage in mind, meaning that at a 2x target, ideally share repurchases will be backloaded.

Conclusion: StandardAero Remains Attractive

The latest reduction in the ownership by Carlyle and GIC is one of several incremental ownership reductions, and I believe that there are more to come. None, however, are related to the appeal of StandardAero and are squarely focused on capitalizing on ownership stakes. With an undervaluation against peers, strong growth in the years ahead, and a share repurchase program, I am upgrading StandardAero stock to strong buy.

data.title: Enbridge: Valued Like An AI Tech Company (Downgrade)
data.description:
Enbridge's (ENB) Q4 earnings release was solid. The company continues demonstrating stable growth in distributable cash flow even though the CapEx program is ramping up aggressively. I was lucky to lock in a notably more attractive dividend yield than the current 5.3% level, as the stock has rallied by 13% since my previous bullish call. Enbridge's fundamental strength makes me confident in holding this winner further. However, it is currently priced to perfection, and I am not ready to buy it while it is that expensive.

On the other hand, there is a good idea for income investors who missed out on the moment when ENB's dividend yield was much higher. The company has various issues of preference shares, and the one that looks particularly interesting is an instrument called 'Cumulative Redeemable Preference Shares Series 1' (EBBGF), which currently offers a much higher 6.7% forward yield compared to common stock. To sum up, I downgrade common stock ENB to a 'Hold' because it is currently priced for perfection while I see increasing risks due to the rapidly expanding CapEx program.

Q4 Earnings Review

The company released its Q4 earnings last Friday, on February 13th. It was a solid quarter as ENB delivered another double earnings beat, highlighting that the Q3 EPS miss was just a lone hiccup and not a trend. The Q4 beat was strong, with a massive $3.3 billion revenue surprise and a $0.08 non-GAAP EPS surprise. Enbridge's adjusted EBITDA increased both on a Q4 (1.6% up) and full-year (7% up) basis, which inevitably positively affected the distributable cash flow ('DCF').

The company's full-year DCF per share increased by 2.6%, which is decent growth given Enbridge's aggressive investments in growth projects in 2025. According to the company's cash flow statement, its net CapEx increased from $4.7 billion in 2024 to $6.5 billion in 2025, a massive 38% increase. Therefore, I believe that the management's capital allocation remains strong as the company continues to invest in growth projects aggressively while maintaining the DCF per share growth. With that being said, I believe that ENB's 5.3% forward dividend yield is safe.

The current yield might be quite attractive for some investors, as it is notably higher than the risk-free rate. However, I have been buying this stock since its dividend yield was above 7%, so for me the 5.3% yield is not that attractive. By the way, for investors who agree with me that ordinary shares' yield is not very attractive, I recommend paying attention to EBBGF, which currently offers a hefty 6.7% yield. These are preferred shares, which means they enjoy superior dividend coverage compared to common shares, as their payouts are prioritized in the company's capital structure. EBBGF currently trades at $24.85 per share and offers a quarterly dividend of $0.41898.

Besides robust Q4 and full-year performance, the management also released solid guidance, which is another bullish sign. The management expects growth in adjusted EBITDA and DCF per share in 2026, which will continue supporting dividend payouts to investors.

On the other hand, there is one warning sign. The company continues aggressively expanding its CapEx backlog as it ended FY2025 with $39 billion in total secured capital program. As of my previous ENB article, which I shared after the Q3 earnings, the capital program amounted to $35 billion. It implies that within just one quarter, Q4, Enbridge's capital program amount increased by more than 10%. Meanwhile, even if the company achieves the higher end of its adjusted FY2026 EBITDA guidance of $20.8 billion, it will be a 4% growth compared to FY2025. The fact that the CapEx budget is growing much more aggressively than the FY2026 adjusted EBITDA projected growth means elevated profitability compression risks for investors over the long term. Of course, Enbridge has a stellar proven record of success, and the industry itself enjoys AI infrastructure tailwinds, but aggressively ramping CapEx is always a big risk due to long payback periods for midstream infrastructure projects.

Valuation Analysis

Enbridge's current P/E ratio of above 20 is high for a midstream company because the industry itself is mature and it is not growing fast. Even if the company achieves the projected EPS growth as outlined in the table below, the estimated FY2029 P/E of almost 19 is still quite high for a midstream company. To give readers more understanding, just picture the fact that NVIDIA's (NVDA) estimated forward P/E ratio for FY2029 is 16.5 at the moment.

Please do not get me wrong here. I know that comparing Enbridge to Nvidia is like comparing apples to oranges. What I want to spotlight here is the fact that sometimes we hear and read opinions that 'AI is a bubble' and 'the semiconductor industry is a bubble.' And here we see that Enbridge's forward P/E ratio trajectory is comparable to the largest representative of the semiconductor industry.

If you don't like the Nvidia comparison, let's be more traditional in selecting benchmarks and compare ENB's valuation against its direct rivals, other large North American energy midstream companies. As the above comparison suggests, ENB is significantly more expensive across almost all metrics. ENB's premium valuation could have been justified if it had unparalleled growth, profitability, or recent EPS revisions. However, the below comparison suggests that ENB's Quant factor grades across these dimensions are comparable to peers. Therefore, its premium valuation is not justified.

Meanwhile, the current $24.85 share price of EBBGF looks attractive as the price is currently slightly cheaper than the $25 par value. Moreover, its dividend yield of 6.7% is twice as high as the sector median of 3.35%, which also underscores the attractiveness of the valuation.

Risks of Investing in EBBGF

While EBBGF's valuation looks good at the moment, it is important to understand that it doesn't mean there is significant upside potential. The fact that it is a redeemable instrument with a $25 par value means that the company can redeem these shares at par at any time. Therefore, the stock is extremely unlikely to ever climb above its par value. It means that EBBGF doesn't offer investors share price appreciation at the moment. With that being said, EBBGF is not a very good investment for people seeking capital gains.

It is also vital to understand that EBBGF is traded OTC, meaning much lower liquidity. The average daily trading volume is around 3,000 shares, while ENB's average daily volume is somewhere around 20 million shares. Therefore, EBBGF investors might face high bid-ask spreads, which might offset some portion of the aforementioned 6.7% dividend yield. Moreover, it might lead to delayed execution of your selling orders as fewer investors actively trade this security.

Conclusion

I believe that ENB is currently priced to perfection, which means that buying it despite robust Q4 earnings and positive FY2026 guidance is not a great deal. I think it is a 'Hold' at the moment. Meanwhile, I suggest investors pay attention to the EBBGF instrument, which is currently traded slightly below par value and offers a compelling 6.7% forward dividend yield. I am not buying EBBGF because I seek capital appreciation as well, and I believe that I am too young (under 40) to pursue high yield only. However, I think that Enbridge's stellar fundamentals support the safety of EBBGF's yield, and this stock is likely to be a good investment opportunity for income investors who are closer to retirement and seek lower risk and lower volatility with superior yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 259: $25,900 Allocated, $2,793.02 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
It wasn’t the greatest week in the market, and the narrative regarding AI continued to impact technology companies, especially those focused on SaaS. The S&P 500 fell -1.4%, while the Nasdaq declined by -2.13% this week. It feels like the new momentum trade is rotation, as investors seem to be shifting their focus to companies that have less of a chance of being disrupted by AI. Capital seems to be flowing to companies that are going to utilize AI to expand margins rather than the companies embedding it into their products. Regardless of how anyone feels about software companies, the narrative can’t be proven or disproven, and the State Street SPDR S&P Software & Services ETF (XSW) is down -20.60%, while individual names like Salesforce (CRM) have declined -25.29% over the past month. I have no idea how long this will last, and even Microsoft (MSFT) is down 15.15%, as not even the megacaps are safe. We’re heading into the end of earnings season, and everyone is looking at NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) after hearing what the megacaps plan on allocating toward CapEx, and if Jensen Huang can deliver, I believe it could help the market regain confidence in the technology trade. I think that the selloff is overdone, and we will look back on this period as a great buying opportunity.

After allocating $25,900 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 259 at $36. The account's profitability increased (7.97%) to $9,978.19, which is a return on invested capital of 38.68%. This week, I added to my positions in the Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (QDVO) and NNN REIT (NNN). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $40 of dividend income in week 259. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $8.72 (0.31%) to $2,793.02. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $5,957.74 of income since its inception, which is 23% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. I can’t believe we’re already halfway through February and earnings season is almost over. Corporate earnings have been strong, and the recent CPI report showed inflation cooling in January to 2.4%. 2026 is off to a bumpy start, but there is a lot of time left in 2026, and I believe we are going to finish the year significantly higher than we are today.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

Over the years, there have been many comments criticizing this portfolio, from the investment style to the number of positions. This is not a technology-heavy portfolio, as it's focused on recurring income and diversification. Currently, many names in the technology sector are having a difficult time, and in a week where the Nasdaq is down -2.13%, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio’s profitability was up 2.23% as another new high was established. My main goals have been to create a portfolio that can mitigate downside risk and generate recurring income. There has been $25,900 allocated toward the portfolio, and it finished the week with a balance of $36,100.69, which is an increase of $10,200.69 (39.38%). The portfolio is now generating $2,793.02 in forward dividend income, and the year is just getting started. This portfolio has navigated several difficult environments and continues to prove that it is built to accomplish my goals. I am looking forward to continuing to allocate capital toward it and building out the ongoing stream of income.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $930.26 (33.31%)
  • Equities $638.77 (22.87%)
  • CEFs $470.49 (16.85%)
  • REITs $476.47 (17.06%)
  • BDCs $277.02 (9.92%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself for 30 years in the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. In 2025, I collected $2,411.27 in dividend income from 831 dividends. After 6 weeks in 2026, $411.03 of dividend income was produced, which is 17.05% of the total income from 2025. There have been 98 dividends generated in 2026, which is 11.79% of the dividends generated in 2025.

There was $40 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $58.72. This is an increase of $12.35 (26.63%) YoY. When I look at the beginning of 2026 compared to 2025, I believe that the income path is going to intensify by a large amount. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

The 2nd week in February is finished, and I have generated $101.29 of income, which is 70.48% of the dividends that were generated last year. I had projected that there was a chance I could reach $200 this month even though it is a short month with a holiday, and I still think that can happen. I’m getting very excited about this portfolio because when I look at the data, February generated the least amount of income in 2025. If I can get close to $200 or exceed it, I believe that the portfolio will be in a position to exceed $250 in monthly income sometime in 2026.

Realty Income (O) has been running and is no longer generating 1 share per year from its dividend income. There are now 39 positions in the table below that are accomplishing this. There's $328.55 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being produced from all the positions generating at least one share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I'm making a strong effort to allocate capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are exceeding 20% of the portfolio again, and it's entirely my fault. I was also adding REITs heading into the rate cut, which caused the portfolio to get a bit top-heavy. It's difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, especially after big tech crushed earnings. I'm going to work on evening out the sectors and having them more proportional in the future. Individual equities now represent 41.30% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.87% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 58.70% of the portfolio and generate 77.13% of the forward income. I'm working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have sectors close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) is starting to run again, and no matter how long I have added capital for without buying more MO, it’s still above 4% of the portfolio. This has been an incredible investment, as it's now been 2 years since I bought shares. My allocation to the top 20 positions looks good in my eyes, as it has been leveling off, and I still believe there is a lot of value to be unlocked. Over the next several months I hope I can have this chart look a bit more even, but I will add to these positions when opportunities present themselves.

This week the top-10 holdings changed again as Citigroup (C) was knocked off the list and AT&T (T) made it back on. This is my favorite table I create because it shows every detail of these investments. For instance, MO has already created 31.30% of the capital I invested from its dividends. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $7,149.74, and they finished the week with a value of $10,378.80. This is an ROI of $3,229.06 (45.16%). There has been $1,596.91 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 22.34% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $758.80 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 10.61%. These positions represent 28.75% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.17% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 259 Additions

This week I added to my positions in the Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF and NNN REIT.

Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF

  • QDVO delivers a compelling blend of double-digit yield and capital appreciation and is outperforming similar ETFs in the sector
  • I am a fan of the tactical covered call strategy as it targets 4–6% option premium plus 0–2% dividend yield, which fits well in the portfolio
  • QDVO’s concentrated portfolio, heavily weighted toward undervalued technology leaders, positions QDVO for continued outperformance as risk-free rates decline

NNN REIT

  • NNN has been breaking out recently, and I think that it is still undervalued as it is trading at less than 13 times its FFO
  • NNN still has a dividend yield of 5.41% that is supported by 35 years of annualized dividend increases
  • NNN is one of my favorite REITs, as its exposure is to single-tenant properties with triple net leases, so all expenses are on the tenant
  • NNN has 10-20 year initial lease terms, with only 8.4% of their leases expiring through 2027, which creates durable revenue

Week 260 Gameplan

It’s that time again, and I need more suggestions for the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. Please leave all of your suggestions in the comment section because I will be adding a position from all of the ideas the community has provided.

Conclusion

February is turning out to be a strong month for dividend income, as I have crossed over the $100 level after the first 2 weeks. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has made new all-time highs and crossed over the $36,000 level for the first time. Part of the technology segment is declining, and many of the high-beta names that retail has allocated capital toward are on a downward descent. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio was built to operate in any investment cycle, and it’s living up to my expectations. With $2,793.02 of dividend income being generated, the account is able to plow more than half of my annualized allocations back into the portfolio. The current market activity is showcasing that sometimes boring investing works, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is at all-time highs while many tech investors are experiencing drawdowns. Please leave all of your comments below, as I enjoy interacting with everyone in the comment section.

data.title: John Hancock 2040 Lifetime Blend Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary

  • Most asset categories produced solid returns in the final three months of 2025, reflecting the favorable backdrop for the world financial markets.
  • The fund posted a gain but slightly underperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to performance, while underlying manager results detracted.

Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The fund's overweight in equities versus bonds contributed to relative performance. An overweight in emerging-market stocks, the best-performing segment of the global equity markets in the quarter, was a further plus. On the other hand, the allocation to defensive equities was a modest detractor at a time in which more speculative stocks were in favor. An overweight in U.S. mid caps also cost the fund some relative performance. Positioning in fixed income had a neutral effect on results. An allocation to emerging-market bonds performed well, as the asset class strongly outperformed its developed-market peers in an environment of falling interest rates and rising commodity prices. Conversely, a position in U.S. Treasury STRIPS detracted amid a broader increase in long-term bond yields.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund's active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets, while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund's positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it's impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.
